brief_document.title: Query service: non-recording read-only dispatch with reply
brief_document.purpose: Let an operator inspect a live workflow's state without recording anything, without blocking the workflow, and without risking determinism. A query is BEAM message passing to the workflow process, answered by a registered handler and replied on a one-shot channel — fast, non-disruptive, and never an event.
brief_document.task: Implement a QueryService that dispatches a read-only query to a workflow process via the engine seam as a distinct message kind, the process answers it from a registered query handler, and the reply returns on a one-shot channel. No Event is ever appended. Handle the failure modes explicitly: unknown query name, reply timeout (engine-configured), and terminal/unknown workflow.

requirements.title: Dispatch a query and return the handler's reply
requirements.spec: WHEN a query is dispatched to a resident workflow, THE SYSTEM SHALL deliver it as a distinct query message kind with a one-shot reply channel and return the reply produced by the workflow's registered query handler.
acceptance: service.rs defines QueryService holding an EngineHandle (and the query timeout from config).
query(workflow_id, name, args) resolves the workflow via the seam, delivers a query message (distinct from a signal message) carrying name + args + a one-shot reply sender, and awaits the reply.
The returned value is whatever the registered query handler produced (a Payload-or-typed reply), surfaced to the caller.
A test (fake seam) wires a fake workflow process that answers a known query and asserts the dispatched query returns the handler's reply.

requirements.title: Queries never record and never block the workflow
requirements.spec: THE SYSTEM SHALL NOT append any Event for a query AND SHALL NOT mutate workflow state or block the workflow's forward progress to answer one.
acceptance: QueryService holds no EventStore and no append path; the type cannot record an event by construction.
The query message is answered at a yield point so it does not preempt in-progress deterministic logic mid-step (documented in the message-kind contract).
A test runs a query against a workflow and asserts zero events were appended to the store over the query's lifetime (using a store observer alongside the fake process).
A doc comment states queries are non-recording and non-disruptive (CO7).

requirements.title: Unknown query name returns a typed error
requirements.spec: IF a query names a handler the workflow does not recognise, THEN THE SYSTEM SHALL return a typed QueryError::UnknownQuery AND the workflow SHALL NOT panic.
acceptance: An unrecognised query name results in QueryError::UnknownQuery(name) returned to the caller.
The workflow process answering an unknown query replies with the error rather than crashing; the workflow process is not torn down by an unknown query.
A test dispatches an unknown query and asserts QueryError::UnknownQuery and that the workflow remains live.

requirements.title: Timeout and terminal/unknown handling
requirements.spec: IF no reply arrives within the engine-configured query timeout, THEN THE SYSTEM SHALL return QueryError::Timeout. IF the target workflow is terminal or unknown, THEN THE SYSTEM SHALL return a typed NotRunning/Unknown error AND SHALL NOT replay the workflow solely to answer the query.
acceptance: The query timeout is supplied by engine configuration (a Duration field/param), NOT a hardcoded constant (CO10).
If the one-shot reply does not arrive within the timeout, query returns QueryError::Timeout (the wait is bounded; tests inject a short timeout and a non-replying fake process).
Querying a Terminal workflow returns QueryError::NotRunning; querying an Unknown workflow returns QueryError::Unknown.
Neither error path triggers a replay of the workflow (the service has no replay capability and does not request one).
Tests assert: non-replying process + short injected timeout -> Timeout; terminal target -> NotRunning; unknown target -> Unknown.

boundaries: SHALL NOT append any event, ever — queries are non-recording (CO7).
SHALL NOT define the Gleam query-handler registration surface — that is AF; this is the engine-side dispatch it binds to.
SHALL NOT define the network query endpoint — that is AW.
SHALL NOT hardcode the query timeout — it is engine-configured (CO10).
verification: cargo test -p aion passes, including timeout/unknown/not-running tests.
QueryService has no EventStore dependency (queries cannot record).
No #[allow], #[expect], or #[ignore] directives in new code.

checklist.text: A query is dispatched to the workflow process as a distinct message kind and answered from a registered query handler, replying on a one-shot reply channel.
No Event is ever appended for a query, and the query never mutates workflow state or blocks the workflow's forward progress.
An unknown query name returns a typed QueryError; the workflow does not panic.

Query dispatch carries an engine-configured timeout and returns QueryError::Timeout if no reply arrives; querying a terminal/unknown workflow returns a typed NotRunning/Unknown error and never replays the workflow solely to answer.

stories.text: As an operator, I want to query a running workflow's current state without recording anything or disturbing its execution so that I can observe progress safely on a live system.
As an operator, I want a query against an unresponsive, terminal, or unknown workflow to return a clear typed error rather than hang so that my tooling stays responsive.

constraints.text: unsafe_code = deny across the cluster's modules.
lib.rs/mod.rs are declarations and re-exports only.
Library errors use thiserror domain types; no anyhow in library code; no unwrap/expect in library code; lock poisoning handled explicitly.

These modules live in the aion crate. The seam with AE (workflow process lifecycle, supervision, module loading, residency resolution) is via an explicit engine-facing handle/trait this cluster consumes; this cluster does not manage process lifecycle.

Every interaction that changes what a workflow observed is recorded as an aion-core Event before/at the point of delivery; queries record nothing. No code path delivers a recordable observation without recording it (no silent observation).

Durable timers go through EventStore::schedule_timer / expired_timers; all AT event appends (TimerStarted/Fired/Cancelled, SignalReceived, ChildWorkflow*) go through the target workflow's single Recorder via the engine seam (AD CO6) — never a direct EventStore::append and no bespoke side-channel persistence outside the aion-store contract.

Tests run against aion-store's InMemoryStore; timer expiry, signal delivery + durability, query reply, child spawn, and all/race/map each have explicit test coverage. Time-dependent tests inject the clock — no wall-clock sleeps in tests.
Timer poll intervals, query timeouts, and recovery-tick cadence are engine-configured, never hardcoded defaults (per the no-assumed-defaults rule).

resolved_context.intention: Define the engine-side mechanisms that let a running workflow process interact with the outside world and coordinate concurrent work: durable timers, signal delivery, read-only queries, child-workflow spawning, and the all/race/map concurrency primitives. A workflow process sleeps for a month and wakes; an external system signals an awaiting workflow and the workflow continues in microseconds; an operator queries a live workflow without perturbing it; a parent spawns children and fans work out across the BEAM. These are the interaction surfaces, and they must be both microsecond-fast on the live path and exactly recoverable after a crash. Every interaction that changes what a workflow observed (a timer firing, a signal arriving, a child completing) is recorded as an aion-core Event so that AD's replay returns the same observation without re-waiting; every interaction that observes without changing (a query) is never recorded. The work here is the live half of durable execution — recording the observable, delivering it over beamr's native mailbox and timer wheel, and recovering it on restart — paired with AD's replay half that reads those same events back.

provenance.quote: Define the engine-side mechanisms that let a running workflow process interact with the outside world and coordinate concurrent work: durable timers, signal delivery, read-only queries, child-workflow
